The pattern seen in the electrocardiogram indicates pulses in a heart beat and their duration. A QRS duration of greater than 110 milliseconds is a diagnostic indication of BBB. There is a unique ECG pattern for blocks in each of the three bundles. Treatment There is no specific therapy for BBB. Patients are usually treated for associated heart diseases. Prognosis The prognosis of blockage in any of the three bundle branches depends on the prognosis of the associated heart disease. The associated diseases determine the outcome of the patient’s health. Occasionally, disruptions in bundle branches lead to complete infranodal A-V block, a more serious blockage of nerve impulses. Lohr, PhD Bunion Definition A bunion is an abnormal enlargement of the joint (the first metatarsophalangeal joint, or MTPJ) at the base of the great or big toe (hallux). It is caused by inflammation and usually results from chronic irritation and pressure from poorly fitting footwear.

abnormal enlargement of the joint (the first metatarsophalangeal joint, or MTPJ) at the base of the great or big toe (hallux). It is caused by inflammation and usually results from chronic irritation and pressure from poorly fitting footwear. Description A displacement of two major bones of the foot (hallux valgus) causes bunions, although not everyone with this displacement will develop the joint swelling and bone overgrowth that characterize a bunion. One of the bones involved is called the first metatarsal bone. This bone is long and slender, with the big toe attached on one GALE ENCYCLOPEDIA OF MEDICINE 2614 Bunion GEM -0433 to 0624 - B 10/22/03 6:09 PM Page 614 end and the other end connected to foot bones closer to the ankle. This foot bone is displaced in the direction of the four other metatarsals connected with the toes. The other bone involved is the big toe itself, which is displaced toward the smaller toes. As the big toe continues to move toward the smaller toes, it may become displaced under or over the second toe. The displacement of these two foot bones causes a projection of bone on the inside portion of the forefoot. The skin over this projection often becomes inflamed from rubbing against the shoe, and a callus may form. The joint contains a small sac (bursa) filled with fluid that cushions the bones and helps the joint to move smoothly. When a bunion forms, this sac becomes inflamed and thickened. The swelling in the joint causes additional pain and pressure in the toe. Causes and symptoms Bunions may form as a result of abnormal motion of the foot during walking or running. One common example of an abnormal movement is an excessive amount of stress placed upon the inside of the foot. This leads to friction and irritation of the involved structures. Age has also been noted as a factor in developing bunions, in part because the underlying bone displacement worsens over time unless corrective measures are taken. Wearing improperly fitting shoes, especially those with a narrow toe box and excessive heel height, often causes the formation of a bunion. This forefoot deformity is seen more often in women than men. The higher frequency in females may be related to the strong link between footwear fashion and bunions.

with a narrow toe box and excessive heel height, often causes the formation of a bunion. This forefoot deformity is seen more often in women than men. The higher frequency in females may be related to the strong link between footwear fashion and bunions. In fact, in a recent survey of more than 350 women, nearly 90% wore shoes that were at least one size too small or too narrow. Because genetic factors can predispose people to the hallux valgus bone displacement, a strong family history of bunions can increase the likelihood of developing this foot disorder. Various arthritic conditions and several genetic and neuromuscular diseases, such as Down syndrome and Marfan syndrome , cause muscle imbalances that can create bunions from displacement of the first metatarsal and big toe. Other possible causes of bunions are leg-length discrepancies, with the bunion present on the longer leg, and trauma occurring to the joint of the big toe. Symptoms of bunions include the common signs of inflammation such as redness, swelling, and pain. The discomfort is primarily located along the inside of the foot just behind the big toe. Because of friction, a callus may develop over the bunion. If an overlapping of the toes is allowed, additional rubbing and pain occurs. Inflammation of this area causes a decrease in motion with associated discomfort in the joint between the big toe and the first metatarsal. If allowed to worsen, the skin over the bunion may break down causing an ulcer, which also presents a problem of potential infection. (Foot ulcers can be particularly dangerous for people with diabetes, who may have trouble feeling the ulcer forming and healing if it becomes infected.) Diagnosis A thorough medical history and physical exam by a physician is always necessary for the proper diagnosis of bunions and other foot conditions. X rays can help confirm the diagnosis by showing the bone displacement, joint swelling, and, in some cases, the overgrowth of bone that characterizes bunions. Doctors will also consider the possibility that the joint pain is caused by or complicated by arthritis (which causes destruction of the cartilage of the joint), gout (which causes the accumulation of uric acid crystals in the joint), tiny fractures of a bone in the foot (stress fractures), or infection and may order additional tests to rule out these possibilities.

Treatment Conservative The first step in treating a bunion is to remove as much pressure from the area as possible. People with bunions should wear shoes that have enough room in the toe box to accommodate the bunion and avoid high-heeled shoes and tight-fitting socks or stockings. Dressings and pads help protect the bunion from additional shoe pressure. The application of splints or customized shoe inserts (orthotics) to correct the alignment of the big toe joint is effective for many bunions. A second-degree burn involves redness, swelling and blistering, and the damage may extend beneath the epidermis to deeper layers of skin (dermis). A thirddegree burn, also called a full-thickness burn, destroys the entire depth of skin, causing significant scarring. Damage also may extend to the underlying fat, muscle, or bone. The severity of the burn is also judged by the amount of body surface area (BSA) involved. Health care workers use the “rule of nines” to determine the percentage of BSA affected in patients more than 9 years old: each arm with its hand is 9% of BSA; each leg with its foot is 18%; the front of the torso is 18%; the back of the torso, including the buttocks, is 18%; the head and neck are 9%; and the genital area (perineum) is 1%. This rule cannot be applied to a young child’s body proportions, so BSA is estimated using the palm of the patient’s hand as a measure of 1% area. The severity of the burn will determine not only the type of treatment, but also where the burn patient should receive treatment. Minor burns may be treated at home or in a doctor’s office.

of 1% area. The severity of the burn will determine not only the type of treatment, but also where the burn patient should receive treatment. Minor burns may be treated at home or in a doctor’s office. These are defined as first- or seconddegree burns covering less than 15% of an adult’s body or less than 10% of a child’s body, or a third-degree burn on less than 2% BSA. Moderate burns should be treated at a hospital. These are defined as first- or second-degree burns covering 15%-25% of an adult’s body or 10%-20% of a child’s body, or a third-degree burn on 2%-10% BSA. Critical, or major, burns are the most serious and should be treated in a specialized burn unit of a hospital. These are defined as first- or second-degree burns covering more than 25% of an adult’s body or more than 20% of a child’s body, or a third-degree burn on more than 10% BSA. In addition, burns involving the hands, feet, face, eyes, ears, or genitals are considered critical. Other factors influence the level of treatment needed, including associated injuries such as bone fractures and smoke inhalation, presence of a chronic disease, or a history of being abused. Also, children and the elderly are more vulnerable to complications from burn injuries and require more intensive care. Causes and symptoms Burns may be caused by even a brief encounter with heat greater than 120°F (49°C). The source of this heat may be the sun (causing a sunburn), hot liquids, steam, fire, electricity, friction (causing rug burns and rope burns), and chemicals (causing a caustic burn upon contact). Diagnosis A physician will diagnose a burn based upon visual examination, and will also ask the patient or family members questions to determine the best treatment. He or she may also check for smoke inhalation, carbon monoxide poisoning, cyanide poisoning, other event-related trauma, or, if suspected, further evidence of child abuse. Treatment Burn treatment consists of relieving pain, preventing infection, and maintaining body fluids, electrolytes, and calorie intake while the body heals. Treatment of chemical or electrical burns is slightly different from the treatment of thermal burns but the objectives are the same. Thermal burn treatment The first act of thermal burn treatment is to stop the burning process. This may be accomplished by letting cool water run over the burned area or by soaking it in cool (not cold) water. Ice should never be applied to the burn. Cool (not cold) wet compresses may provide some pain relief when applied to small areas of first- and second-degree burns. Butter, shortening, or similar salve should never be applied to the burn since it prevents heat from escaping and drives the burning process deeper into the skin. If the burn is minor, it may be cleaned gently with soap and water. Blisters should not be broken. If the skin of the burned area is unbroken and it is not likely to be further irritated by pressure or friction, the burn should be left exposed to the air to promote healing. If the skin is broken or apt to be disturbed, the burned area should be coated lightly with an antibacterial ointment and covered with a sterile bandage. Aspirin , acetaminophen (Tylenol), or ibuprofen (Advil) may be taken to ease pain and relieve inflammation. A doctor should be consulted if these signs of infection appear: increased warmth, redness, pain, or swelling; pus or similar drainage from the wound; swollen lymph nodes; or red streaks spreading away from the burn. In situations where a person has received moderate or critical burns, lifesaving measures take precedence over burn treatment and emergency medical assistance must be called.

drainage from the wound; swollen lymph nodes; or red streaks spreading away from the burn. In situations where a person has received moderate or critical burns, lifesaving measures take precedence over burn treatment and emergency medical assistance must be called. A person with serious burns may stop breathing, and artificial respiration (also called mouth-to-mouth resuscitation or rescue breathing) should be administered immediately. Also, a person with burns covering more than 12% BSA is likely to go into shock; this condition may be prevented by laying the person flat and elevating the feet about 12 in (30 cm). Burned arms and hands should also be raised higher than the person’s heart. In rescues, a blanket may be used to smother any flames as the person is removed from danger. The person whose clothing is on fire should “stop, drop, and roll” or be assisted in lying flat on the ground and rolling to put out the fire. Afterwards, only burnt clothing that comes off easily should be removed; any clothing embedded in the burn should not be disturbed. Removing any smoldering apparel and covering the person with a light, cool, wet cloth, such as a sheet but not a blanket or towel, will stop the burning process. At the hospital, the staff will provide further medical treatment. A tube to aid breathing may be inserted if the patient’s airways or lungs have been damaged, as can happen during an explosion or a fire in a enclosed space. Also, because burns dramatically deplete the body of fluids, replacement fluids are administered intravenously. The patient is also given antibiotics intravenously to prevent infection, and he or she may also receive a tetanus shot, depending on his or her immunization history. Once the burned area is cleaned and treated with antibiotic cream or ointment, it is covered in sterile bandages, which are changed two to three times a day. Surgical removal of dead tissue ( debridement) also takes place. As the burns heal, thick, taut scabs (eschar) form, which the doctor may have to cut to improve blood flow to the more elastic healthy tissue beneath. The patient will also undergo physical and GALE ENCYCLOPEDIA OF MEDICINE 2618 Burns Classification Of Burns First-Degree (Minor) The burned area is painful. The outer skin is reddened. Slight swelling is present. Second-Degree (Moderate) The burned area is painful. The underskin is affected. Blisters may form.

cut to improve blood flow to the more elastic healthy tissue beneath. The patient will also undergo physical and GALE ENCYCLOPEDIA OF MEDICINE 2618 Burns Classification Of Burns First-Degree (Minor) The burned area is painful. The outer skin is reddened. Slight swelling is present. Second-Degree (Moderate) The burned area is painful. The underskin is affected. Blisters may form. The area may have a wet, shin y appearance because of exposed tissue. Third-Degree (Critical) The burned area is insensitive due to the destruction of nerve endings. Skin is destroyed.Muscle tissues and bone underneath may be damaged. The area may be charred, white, or grayish in color. GEM -0433 to 0624 - B 10/22/03 6:09 PM Page 618 occupational therapy to keep the burned areas from becoming inflexible and to minimize scarring. In cases where the skin has been so damaged that it cannot properly heal, a skin graft is usually performed. A skin graft involves taking a piece of skin from an unburned portion of the patient’s body (autograft) and transplanting it to the burned area. When doctors cannot immediately use the patient’s own skin, a temporary graft is performed using the skin of a human donor (allograft), either alive or dead, or the skin of an animal (xenograft), usually that of a pig. The burn victim also may be placed in a hyperbaric chamber, if one is available. In a hyperbaric chamber (which can be a specialized room or enclosed space), the patient is exposed to pure oxygen under high pressure, which can aid in healing. However, for this therapy to be effective, the patient must be placed in a chamber within 24 hours of being burned. Chemical burn treatment Burns from liquid chemicals must be rinsed with cool water for at least 15 minutes to stop the burning process. Any burn to the eye must be similarly flushed with water. In cases of burns from dry chemicals such as lime, the powder should be completely brushed away before the area is washed. Any clothing which may have absorbed the chemical should be removed. The burn should then be loosely covered with a sterile gauze pad and the person taken to the hospital for further treatment. A physician may be able to neutralize the offending chemical with another before treating the burn like a thermal burn of similar severity.

chemical should be removed. The burn should then be loosely covered with a sterile gauze pad and the person taken to the hospital for further treatment. A physician may be able to neutralize the offending chemical with another before treating the burn like a thermal burn of similar severity. Electrical burn treatment Before electrical burns are treated at the site of the accident, the power source must be disconnected if possible and the victim moved away from it to keep the person giving aid from being electrocuted. Lifesaving measures again take priority over burn treatment, so breathing must be checked and assisted if necessary. Electrical burns should be loosely covered with sterile gauze pads and the person taken to the hospital for further treatment. Alternative treatment In addition to the excellent treatment of burns provided by traditional medicine, some alternative approaches may be helpful as well. (Major burns should always be treated by a medical practitioner.) The homeopathic remedies Cantharis and Causticum can assist in burn healing. A number of botanical remedies, applied topically, can also help burns heal. These include aloe KEY TERMS Debridement —The surgical removal of dead tissue. Dermis—The basal layer of skin; it contains blood and lymphatic vessels, nerves, glands, and hair follicles. Epidermis—The outer portion of skin, made up of four or five superficial layers. Shock—An abnormal condition resulting from low blood volume due to hemorrhage or dehydration. Signs of shock include rapid pulse and breathing, and cool, moist, pale skin. (Aloe barbadensis ), oil of St.-John’s-wort ( Hypericum perforatum), calendula ( Calendula officinalis ), comfrey (Symphytum officinale), and tea tree oil (Melaleuca spp.). Supplementing the diet with vitamin C, vitamin E, and zinc also is beneficial for wound healing. Prognosis The prognosis is dependent upon the degree of the burn, the amount of body surface covered, whether critical body parts were affected, any additional injuries or complications like infection, and the promptness of medical treatment. Minor burns may heal in five to 10 days with no scarring. Moderate burns may heal in 10-14 days and may leave scarring. Critical or major burns take more than 14 days to heal and will leave significant scarring. Scar tissue may limit mobility and functionality, but physical therapy may overcome these limitations. In some cases, additional surgery may be advisable to remove scar tissue and restore appearance. Prevention Burns are commonly received in residential fires.

and may leave scarring. Critical or major burns take more than 14 days to heal and will leave significant scarring. Scar tissue may limit mobility and functionality, but physical therapy may overcome these limitations. In some cases, additional surgery may be advisable to remove scar tissue and restore appearance. Prevention Burns are commonly received in residential fires. Properly placed and working smoke detectors in combination with rapid evacuation plans will minimize a person’s exposure to smoke and flames in the event of a fire. Children must be taught never to play with matches, lighters, fireworks, gasoline, and cleaning fluids. Burns by scalding with hot water or other liquids may be prevented by setting the water heater thermostat no higher than 120°F (49°C), checking the temperature of bath water before getting into the tub, and turning pot handles on the stove out of the reach of children. Care should be used when removing covers from pans of GALE ENCYCLOPEDIA OF MEDICINE 2 619 Burns GEM -0433 to 0624 - B 10/22/03 6:09 PM Page 619 the affected joint area is the first step. A sling can be used for a shoulder injury; a cane is helpful for hip problems. The patient can take nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like aspirin, ibuprofin, and naproxen. They can be obtained without a prescription and relieve the pain and inflammation. Once the pain decreases, exercises of the affected area can begin. If the nearby muscles have become weak because of the disease or prolonged immobility, then exercises to build strength and improve movement are best. A doctor or physical therapist can prescribe an effective regimen. If the bursitis is related to an inflammatory condition like arthritis or gout, then management of that disease is needed to control the bursitis. When bursitis does not respond to conservative treatment, an injection into the joint of a long-acting corticosteroid preparation, like prednisone, can bring immediate and lasting relief. A corticosteroid is a hormonal substance that is the most effective drug for reducing inflammation. The drug is mixed with a local anesthetic and works on the joint within five minutes. Usually one injection is all that is needed. Surgery to remove the damaged bursa may be performed in extreme cases. If the bursitis is caused by an infection, then additional treatment is needed. Septic bursitis is caused by the presence of a pus-forming organism, usually staphylococcus aureus.

local anesthetic and works on the joint within five minutes. Usually one injection is all that is needed. Surgery to remove the damaged bursa may be performed in extreme cases. If the bursitis is caused by an infection, then additional treatment is needed. Septic bursitis is caused by the presence of a pus-forming organism, usually staphylococcus aureus. This is confirmed by examining a sample of the fluid in the bursa and requires treatment with antibiotics taken by mouth, injected into a muscle or into a vein (intravenously). The bursa will also need to be drained by needle two or three times over the first week of treatment. When a patient has such a serious infection, there may be underlying causes. There could be undiscovered diabetes, or an inefficient immune system caused by human immunodeficiencyvirus infection (HIV). Alternative treatment Alternative treatments take into consideration the role of diet in causing bursitis. The faulty use of calcium by the body, magnesium deficiency, and food allergies may have a role. Diet changes and vitamin supplements may be helpful. The use of herbs, homeopathy , aromatherapy , and hydrotherapy can help relieve symptoms. Ginger is useful in reducing inflammation. Acupuncture has been proven effective in treating hip and shoulder pain caused by bursitis and other conditions. Other therapies that deal effectively with musculoskeletal problems (relating to the muscles and skeleton), may also be helpful, such as body work, magnetic field therapy , naturopathic medicine , chiropractic , and applied kinesiology. KEY TERMS Arthritis—Inflammation of a joint that may lead to changes in the joint’s structure. It causes pain and swelling. Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic disease that leads to crippling deformities. Diabetes mellitus —A metabolic disease caused by a deficiency of insulin, which is essential to process carbohydrates in the body. Gout—A hereditary metabolic disease that is a form of arthritis and causes inflammation of the joints. It is more common in men. Inflammation—The reaction of tissue to injury. Kinesiology—The science or study of movement. Prognosis Bursitis usually responds well to treatment, but it may develop into a chronic condition if the underlying cause is not corrected. Prevention Aggravating factors should be eliminated to prevent bursitis. Overexercising or the repetition of a movement that triggers the condition should be avoided. Doing exercises to strengthen the muscles around the joint will also help.

may develop into a chronic condition if the underlying cause is not corrected. Prevention Aggravating factors should be eliminated to prevent bursitis. Overexercising or the repetition of a movement that triggers the condition should be avoided. Doing exercises to strengthen the muscles around the joint will also help. When doing repetitive tasks, frequent breaks should be taken and the activity should be alternated with others using different parts of the body. To cushion the joints, it is a good idea to use cushioned chairs when sitting and foam kneeling pads for the knees. Leaning on the elbows, kneeling or sitting on a hard surface for a long period of time should be avoided. Not wearing high heels can help prevent bursitis in the heel, as can changing to new running shoes as soon as the old ones are worn out. Resources BOOKS Bennett, J. Claude, and Fred Plum, eds. Hypertension affects approximately 1.28 billion adults worldwide and is the leading modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease and premature death. The 2020 International Society of Hypertension guidelines define hypertension as systolic blood pressure ≥140 mmHg or diastolic ≥90 mmHg on two separate occasions. Optimal blood pressure is below 120/80 mmHg. Lifestyle interventions — including sodium restriction to <5g/day, DASH diet, aerobic exercise 150 min/week, weight reduction, smoking cessation, and alcohol moderation — are first-line for all patients and can reduce systolic BP by 5–20 mmHg.
source: Global Hypertension Practice Guidelines — PMID 33268196 | Hypertension. 2020

text: Antihypertensive drug therapy should be initiated when blood pressure exceeds 140/90 mmHg in low-risk patients or 130/80 mmHg in high-risk patients (diabetes, CKD, established CVD). First-line agents include ACE inhibitors (ramipril, lisinopril), ARBs (losartan, valsartan), calcium channel blockers (amlodipine), and thiazide diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide, chlorthalidone). ACE inhibitors and ARBs are preferred in patients with diabetes mellitus or proteinuric CKD due to renoprotective effects. Combination therapy achieves target BP more effectively than monotherapy dose escalation.
source: Pharmacological Treatment of Hypertension — PMID 31475488 | Lancet. 2019

text: Resistant hypertension is defined as blood pressure remaining above goal despite concurrent use of three antihypertensive agents of different classes, including a diuretic, at maximally tolerated doses. Prevalence is approximately 10–15% of treated hypertensive patients. Secondary causes including primary hyperaldosteronism, renal artery stenosis, obstructive sleep apnea, and pheochromocytoma must be excluded. Spironolactone added as a fourth agent provides the greatest BP reduction in true resistant hypertension, reducing systolic BP by a mean of 11 mmHg.

Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a progressive metabolic disorder characterised by hyperglycaemia resulting from insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency. Global prevalence has tripled since 1980 to 537 million adults in 2021. Diagnosis requires fasting plasma glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L (126 mg/dL), 2-hour glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L on OGTT, HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%), or random glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L with symptoms. Lifestyle modification — structured physical activity, caloric restriction, and 5–10% weight loss — can delay or prevent progression from prediabetes to T2DM.
source: Global Epidemiology of Type 2 Diabetes — PMID 34161073 | Lancet. 2021

text: Metformin remains the preferred initial glucose-lowering agent for most patients with type 2 diabetes due to its established efficacy (HbA1c reduction 1–1.5%), cardiovascular safety, weight neutrality, low hypoglycaemia risk, and low cost. It acts by reducing hepatic glucose production and improving peripheral insulin sensitivity via AMPK activation. GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide, dulaglutide) and SGLT-2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin, canagliflozin) are preferred second-line agents in patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease.
source: Management of Hyperglycaemia in Type 2 Diabetes — PMID 30291128 | Diabetologia. 2019

text: SGLT-2 inhibitors reduce cardiovascular mortality by 38%, hospitalisation for heart failure by 35%, and progression of kidney disease by 45% in patients with type 2 diabetes and established cardiovascular disease. Empagliflozin, canagliflozin, and dapagliflozin have all demonstrated cardiovascular and renal benefits in large randomised trials (EMPA-REG OUTCOME, CANVAS, DECLARE-TIMI 58). Adverse effects include urogenital infections, diabetic ketoacidosis (rare), and Fournier's gangrene (rare). They should be used with caution in patients with eGFR <45 mL/min/1.73m².
source: SGLT-2 Inhibitors Cardiovascular and Renal Benefits — PMID 33053784 | NEJM. 2020

text: Semaglutide, a GLP-1 receptor agonist, reduces HbA1c by 1.5–1.8% and body weight by 4–6 kg when used as weekly subcutaneous injection. The SUSTAIN-6 trial demonstrated a 26% reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) versus placebo. Oral semaglutide (Rybelsus) provides comparable glycaemic and weight benefits. Common side effects include nausea, vomiting, and diarrhoea, typically self-limiting. GLP-1 RAs are contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN-2 syndrome.
source: Semaglutide in Type 2 Diabetes — PMID 29677303 | NEJM. 2018

text: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF, LVEF <40%) affects 50% of heart failure patients and carries a 5-year mortality exceeding 50%. The four pillars of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) that reduce mortality are: ACE inhibitors/ARBs or ARNi (sacubitril/valsartan), beta-blockers (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol), mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone), and SGLT-2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin). Sacubitril/valsartan (Entresto) reduces cardiovascular death and HF hospitalisation by 20% compared to enalapril.
source: 2021 ESC Heart Failure Guidelines — PMID 33215916 | Eur Heart J. 2021

text: Acute decompensated heart failure (ADHF) presenting with fluid overload requires prompt intravenous diuresis with furosemide. Initial IV furosemide dose should be at least equivalent to the patient's oral maintenance dose. SGLT-2 inhibitors accelerate decongestion and reduce 30-day rehospitalisation when initiated during ADHF admission. BNP or NT-proBNP guided therapy helps optimise diuretic dosing. Early initiation of neurohormonal antagonists (ACEi/ARB/ARNi, beta-blockers, MRA) before discharge improves post-discharge outcomes.
source: Acute Heart Failure Management — PMID 31535104 | JACC. 2019

text: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, affecting 37.5 million people globally. It increases stroke risk 5-fold. Stroke risk stratification using the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score guides anticoagulation decisions: score ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women warrants oral anticoagulation. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs — apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are preferred over warfarin due to superior efficacy, reduced intracranial bleeding, and no requirement for routine INR monitoring.
source: 2020 ESC Atrial Fibrillation Guidelines — PMID 31504429 | Eur Heart J. 2020

text: Rate control versus rhythm control strategies in atrial fibrillation have been debated for two decades. The EAST-AFNET 4 trial demonstrated that early rhythm control (within 1 year of AF diagnosis) significantly reduced cardiovascular death, stroke, and hospitalisation compared to usual care. Rhythm control options include antiarrhythmic drugs (flecainide, propafenone for structurally normal hearts; amiodarone, sotalol for structural heart disease) and catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation), which achieves superior rhythm control with 65–70% freedom from AF at 1 year.
source: Early Rhythm Control in Atrial Fibrillation — PMID 32865373 | NEJM. 2020

text: Asthma is a heterogeneous chronic airway disease affecting 358 million people worldwide, characterised by variable airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and type 2 airway inflammation. Diagnosis requires demonstration of reversible airflow obstruction: ≥12% and 200 mL improvement in FEV1 after short-acting bronchodilator, or positive bronchial challenge test. Type 2 high asthma (elevated eosinophils, FeNO ≥25 ppb, or IgE sensitisation) responds best to inhaled corticosteroids and biologic therapies targeting IL-4, IL-5, and IL-13 pathways.
source: GINA Asthma Guidelines 2021 — PMID 32979942 | Eur Respir J. 2020

text: Asthma management follows a 5-step treatment ladder. Steps 1–2 use low-dose inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) with as-needed SABA or as-needed ICS-formoterol. Steps 3–4 add long-acting beta-agonists (LABA) to ICS, with medium-high ICS doses. Step 5 uses high-dose ICS-LABA plus add-on biologics for severe eosinophilic asthma: mepolizumab and benralizumab target IL-5, dupilumab targets IL-4/IL-13, omalizumab targets IgE. Biologic therapy reduces exacerbations by 50–60% and achieves oral corticosteroid-sparing in severe asthma.

Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) is characterised by persistent airflow limitation due to airway and/or alveolar abnormalities, caused primarily by significant exposure to noxious particles or gases, usually cigarette smoke. Diagnosis requires post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 on spirometry. GOLD classification grades severity GOLD 1–4 by FEV1 % predicted. Smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention to slow disease progression, reducing FEV1 decline from 60 mL/year to 30 mL/year.
source: GOLD COPD Report 2021 — PMID 32877039 | Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2021

text: Long-acting bronchodilators (LABA and LAMA) are the cornerstone of COPD pharmacotherapy. Dual bronchodilation with LABA-LAMA combination (umeclidinium/vilanterol, tiotropium/olodaterol, glycopyrrolate/formoterol) reduces exacerbations and improves lung function more than monotherapy. Triple therapy (ICS-LABA-LAMA) is reserved for patients with frequent exacerbations and blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/µL. Roflumilast (PDE-4 inhibitor) reduces exacerbations in severe COPD with chronic bronchitis. Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise capacity, dyspnoea, and quality of life.
source: Pharmacological Management of COPD — PMID 31727409 | NEJM. 2019

text: Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is the leading infectious cause of death worldwide. Streptococcus pneumoniae accounts for 30–40% of cases requiring hospitalisation. Severity assessment using validated tools — CURB-65 (confusion, urea, respiratory rate, blood pressure, age ≥65) or PSI/PORT score — guides site-of-care decisions. CURB-65 score 0–1: outpatient treatment; score 2: consider hospitalisation; score ≥3: hospitalise, consider ICU. Blood cultures should be obtained before antibiotics in hospitalised patients.

Empirical antibiotic selection for community-acquired pneumonia follows site-of-care and severity. Outpatients without comorbidities: amoxicillin 1g TDS or doxycycline. Outpatients with comorbidities: amoxicillin-clavulanate plus macrolide, or respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin, moxifloxacin). Hospitalised non-ICU patients: beta-lactam plus macrolide or respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy. ICU patients: beta-lactam plus either azithromycin or fluoroquinolone. Duration: 5 days for mild-moderate CAP if clinical stability achieved. COVID-19 pneumonia requires specific management pathway.
source: Antibiotic Therapy for CAP — PMID 33275120 | IDSA Guidelines. 2020

text: Sepsis is defined as life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection (Sepsis-3 definition). Organ dysfunction is identified by an acute increase in SOFA score ≥2 points. Septic shock is a subset with vasopressor requirement to maintain MAP ≥65 mmHg and lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation. In-hospital mortality for septic shock exceeds 40%. Early recognition using qSOFA (altered mentation, RR ≥22/min, SBP ≤100 mmHg) at the bedside facilitates timely intervention.

The Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1 Bundle recommends: measure lactate (re-measure if >2 mmol/L), obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, begin 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L, and apply vasopressors for refractory hypotension targeting MAP ≥65 mmHg. Norepinephrine is the first-choice vasopressor. Vasopressin (0.03 U/min) is added second. Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day is reserved for refractory septic shock. Source control (drainage, debridement) must be achieved within 6–12 hours.
source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Bundle — PMID 30635113 | Intensive Care Med. 2019

text: Major depressive disorder (MDD) affects 280 million people worldwide and is the leading cause of disability. DSM-5 diagnosis requires ≥5 of 9 symptoms present for ≥2 weeks, including depressed mood or anhedonia: depressed mood, anhedonia, weight/appetite change, insomnia or hypersomnia, psychomotor agitation/retardation, fatigue, worthlessness/guilt, poor concentration, recurrent thoughts of death. PHQ-9 score ≥10 indicates moderate-severe depression warranting treatment. Suicide risk must be assessed at every visit.

First-line pharmacotherapy for major depression includes SSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine) and SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine). SSRIs are preferred for initial treatment due to tolerability and safety in overdose. Adequate antidepressant trial requires ≥4–6 weeks at therapeutic dose. Response rates are 50–60%; remission 30–40% with first agent. Non-response warrants dose increase, augmentation (lithium, atypical antipsychotics, buspirone), or switch. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is equally effective to antidepressants for mild-moderate depression and superior for relapse prevention.

Treatment-resistant depression (TRD) is defined as failure to respond to ≥2 adequate antidepressant trials. Esketamine (Spravato) nasal spray is FDA-approved for TRD and MDD with acute suicidal ideation, demonstrating rapid onset within hours. Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) remains the most effective treatment for severe TRD, achieving remission in 60–80% of patients. Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) is a non-invasive alternative with 30–35% remission rates. Psychedelic-assisted psychotherapy with psilocybin shows promising results in early clinical trials.

Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) is characterised by excessive, difficult-to-control worry about multiple domains lasting ≥6 months, accompanied by ≥3 of: restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance. Lifetime prevalence is 5–6%. GAD-7 score ≥10 indicates moderate severity. First-line treatments are SSRIs/SNRIs (escitalopram, venlafaxine, duloxetine) and cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT). Benzodiazepines should be avoided as first-line due to dependence risk; reserved for short-term acute management.

Ischaemic stroke constitutes 87% of all strokes and is caused by thrombotic or embolic occlusion of cerebral arteries. Rapid recognition using FAST (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency) enables timely reperfusion therapy. IV alteplase (tPA) remains standard of care within 4.5 hours of symptom onset in eligible patients, reducing disability by 30%. Mechanical thrombectomy is indicated for large vessel occlusion (LVO) within 6–24 hours and dramatically improves outcomes, with number needed to treat of 2.6 for functional independence.

Secondary stroke prevention depends on stroke mechanism. Cardioembolic stroke (AF, valvular disease): long-term anticoagulation with DOAC. Non-cardioembolic ischaemic stroke/TIA: dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days then single antiplatelet long-term; high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40–80 mg) targeting LDL <1.8 mmol/L; blood pressure control <130/80 mmHg. Carotid endarterectomy or stenting for symptomatic carotid stenosis ≥70% within 2 weeks of TIA/minor stroke reduces recurrent stroke risk by 50%.

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common cause of dementia, accounting for 60–70% of cases globally, affecting 55 million people. Pathological hallmarks include amyloid-β plaques and neurofibrillary tau tangles. Diagnosis is increasingly biomarker-based: amyloid PET, tau PET, CSF Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio, and plasma phospho-tau 217 (p-tau217). Clinical staging: mild cognitive impairment (MCI) due to AD → mild AD dementia → moderate → severe. MMSE and MoCA are validated screening tools. APOE ε4 allele is the strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset AD.
source: Alzheimer's Disease Diagnosis and Management — PMID 32640687 | Lancet. 2020

text: Lecanemab and donanemab are anti-amyloid monoclonal antibodies that significantly reduce amyloid burden and slow cognitive decline in early AD. Lecanemab reduced clinical decline by 27% versus placebo in the CLARITY AD trial and received FDA approval in 2023. Amyloid-related imaging abnormalities (ARIA), including oedema (ARIA-E) and microhaemorrhages (ARIA-H), are important safety concerns requiring MRI monitoring. Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and memantine provide modest symptomatic benefit across all stages but do not modify disease progression.
source: Disease-Modifying Therapy in Alzheimer's Disease — PMID 34129181 | NEJM. 2022

text: Breast cancer is the most common cancer in women globally, with 2.3 million new cases in 2020. Risk factors include BRCA1/2 mutations (lifetime risk 50–70%), age, dense breast tissue, early menarche, late menopause, nulliparity, HRT use, and alcohol. Screening mammography every 1–2 years from age 40–50 (guidelines vary) reduces breast cancer mortality by 15–20%. Tumour characterisation: hormone receptor (HR) status (ER, PR), HER2 status, and Ki-67 proliferation index guide treatment selection.
source: Breast Cancer Epidemiology and Screening — PMID 31851796 | Lancet. 2019

text: Treatment of early-stage breast cancer combines surgery (breast-conserving surgery + radiotherapy equivalent to mastectomy for stages I–II), systemic therapy based on molecular subtype. HR+/HER2- tumours: adjuvant endocrine therapy (tamoxifen 5–10 years for premenopausal; aromatase inhibitors for postmenopausal) ± CDK4/6 inhibitors (abemaciclib) for high-risk. HER2+ tumours: trastuzumab + pertuzumab-based chemotherapy. Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC): neoadjuvant chemotherapy ± immunotherapy (pembrolizumab for PD-L1+, olaparib for BRCA-mutated).
source: Early Breast Cancer Treatment Guidelines — PMID 33207254 | NEJM. 2020

text: Lung cancer remains the leading cause of cancer mortality worldwide, with 1.8 million deaths annually. Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) accounts for 85% of cases. Low-dose CT screening annually in high-risk individuals (age 50–80, ≥20 pack-year smoking history, current or quit within 15 years) reduces lung cancer mortality by 20–24%. Molecular profiling of advanced NSCLC for actionable mutations — EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E, MET, RET, NTRK, KRAS G12C — is mandatory before initiating therapy as targeted therapies vastly outperform chemotherapy.
source: Lung Cancer Screening and Molecular Profiling — PMID 30280641 | NEJM. 2018

text: Immunotherapy has transformed advanced NSCLC management. Pembrolizumab monotherapy is first-line for PD-L1 ≥50% tumours without actionable mutations (OS 16 months vs 11 months for chemotherapy). Pembrolizumab + platinum-based chemotherapy benefits all PD-L1 expression levels. Nivolumab + ipilimumab improves OS regardless of PD-L1 status. Checkpoint inhibitor-related immune adverse events (irAEs) — pneumonitis, colitis, endocrinopathies, hepatitis — require prompt recognition and steroid management. Osimertinib is standard for EGFR-mutant NSCLC, achieving PFS of 18 months.
source: Immunotherapy in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer — PMID 33658772 | Lancet Oncol. 2021

text: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is the third most common cancer and second leading cause of cancer death. Screening reduces CRC mortality by detecting precancerous adenomas and early-stage cancers. Colonoscopy every 10 years from age 45 (USPSTF 2021 recommendation) is the gold standard — allows direct visualisation, biopsy, and polypectomy. Alternatives: annual high-sensitivity guaiac FOBT or FIT, stool DNA test (Cologuard) every 1–3 years, CT colonography every 5 years. Patients with Lynch syndrome or FAP require surveillance from age 20–25.
source: Colorectal Cancer Screening Guidelines — PMID 31189536 | JAMA. 2019

text: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic systemic autoimmune disease characterised by symmetric polyarthritis, synovial inflammation, and progressive joint destruction. RF and anti-CCP antibodies are positive in 60–80% of cases (seropositive RA). Treat-to-target strategy aiming for DAS28 remission (<2.6) or low disease activity (<3.2) improves long-term outcomes. Methotrexate (MTX) 15–25 mg/week is the anchor DMARD for most patients. Hydroxychloroquine and sulfasalazine are added for triple DMARD therapy.
source: RA Treatment Guidelines — PMID 32659001 | Ann Rheum Dis. 2020

text: Biologic DMARDs are indicated for RA patients with inadequate response to conventional DMARDs. TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, certolizumab, golimumab, infliximab) are first-line biologics. IL-6 receptor inhibitors (tocilizumab, sarilumab) are alternatives, particularly when elevated CRP/ESR is a clinical concern. JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, baricitinib, upadacitinib) are oral targeted synthetic DMARDs equally effective to biologics. All biologics and JAK inhibitors require screening for TB, hepatitis B, and vaccinations before initiation. Cardiovascular risk assessment is essential given accelerated atherosclerosis in RA.
source: Biologic Therapy in Rheumatoid Arthritis — PMID 30765264 | NEJM. 2019

text: Osteoporosis is defined by a bone mineral density (BMD) T-score ≤−2.5 at lumbar spine or femoral neck on DXA. FRAX tool calculates 10-year fracture probability incorporating BMD and clinical risk factors. Treatment thresholds: prior fragility fracture, T-score ≤−2.5, or FRAX major osteoporotic fracture risk ≥20%. Bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid) are first-line, reducing vertebral fractures by 60–70% and hip fractures by 40–50%. Calcium 1000–1200 mg/day and vitamin D 800–1000 IU/day are essential adjuncts.
source: Osteoporosis Diagnosis and Management — PMID 33602346 | Lancet. 2021

text: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is projected to cause 10 million deaths annually by 2050, surpassing cancer. ESKAPE pathogens (Enterococcus faecium, Staphylococcus aureus, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Acinetobacter baumannii, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Enterobacter species) cause the majority of hospital-acquired infections resistant to standard antibiotics. MRSA (methicillin-resistant S. aureus) requires vancomycin or daptomycin. Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE) require ceftazidime-avibactam, meropenem-vaborbactam, or cefiderocol. Antibiotic stewardship programmes reduce AMR rates, C. difficile infections, and hospital costs.

HIV infection affects 38 million people globally. Antiretroviral therapy (ART) suppresses viral replication to undetectable levels, restores immune function, and enables near-normal life expectancy. Universal ART initiation regardless of CD4 count is now standard of care. Preferred first-line regimen: bictegravir/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine (Biktarvy) — a single-tablet once-daily regimen with high efficacy, barrier to resistance, and favourable safety profile. Undetectable = Untransmittable (U=U): viral load <200 copies/mL eliminates sexual HIV transmission.
source: HIV Antiretroviral Therapy Guidelines — PMID 32220651 | Lancet. 2020

text: COVID-19 ranges from asymptomatic infection to critical illness. Risk factors for severe disease: age >65, obesity, diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, chronic lung disease, immunosuppression. WHO clinical severity: mild (no hypoxia), moderate (SpO2 90–94%), severe (SpO2 <90% or RR >30), critical (ARDS, septic shock). Dexamethasone 6 mg/day for 10 days reduces 28-day mortality in patients requiring supplemental oxygen or mechanical ventilation (RECOVERY trial, RR 0.83). Therapeutic anticoagulation benefits moderately ill hospitalised patients not requiring ICU-level care.

Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) reduces risk of hospitalisation or death by 89% in high-risk non-hospitalised adults with COVID-19 when initiated within 5 days of symptom onset. Remdesivir reduces hospitalisation by 87% in outpatients when started within 7 days. Molnupiravir reduces risk of hospitalisation/death by 30%. COVID-19 vaccines (mRNA platforms: BNT162b2, mRNA-1273) demonstrate 90–95% efficacy against severe disease and death. Hybrid immunity (vaccination + prior infection) provides broadest protection against variants. Long COVID affects 10–30% of those infected, causing fatigue, cognitive impairment, and dyspnoea persisting >12 weeks.

Hypothyroidism affects 5% of adults, more common in women and those >60 years. Primary hypothyroidism shows elevated TSH with low free T4. Hashimoto's thyroiditis (autoimmune) is the most common cause in iodine-sufficient regions, confirmed by elevated TPO antibodies. Levothyroxine is the treatment of choice, starting at 1.6 µg/kg/day (lower in elderly/cardiac patients). Target TSH 0.5–2.5 mIU/L for most patients; 0.1–1.5 mIU/L during pregnancy. Dose adjustments based on TSH every 6–8 weeks until stable, then annually.

Hyperthyroidism presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, palpitations, tremor, and anxiety. Graves' disease (TSH receptor antibodies) causes 70–80% of cases. Treatment options: antithyroid drugs (methimazole/carbimazole, propylthiouracil) to achieve remission in 30–50% after 12–18 months; radioactive iodine (RAI) therapy most cost-effective; thyroidectomy for large goitre, suspected malignancy, or RAI contraindication. Beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol) control adrenergic symptoms immediately. Thyroid storm is a life-threatening emergency requiring ICU admission, high-dose antithyroid drugs, beta-blockade, steroids, and iodine solution.

Migraine affects 1 billion people worldwide and is the second leading cause of disability globally. Classified as episodic (<15 headache days/month) or chronic (≥15 days/month). Acute treatment: triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan) are first-line for moderate-severe attacks, providing pain freedom in 30–40% at 2 hours. CGRP receptor antagonists (gepants: ubrogepant, rimegepant) and 5-HT1F agonists (ditans: lasmiditan) are newer acute options without vasoconstrictive effects, suitable for patients with cardiovascular contraindications to triptans.
source: Migraine Acute Treatment — PMID 31504404 | Lancet Neurol. 2019

text: Migraine prevention is indicated for ≥4 headache days/month or significant disability. Traditional preventives: topiramate, valproate, beta-blockers (propranolol, metoprolol), amitriptyline, venlafaxine. Anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab) represent a major advance in preventive therapy, reducing monthly migraine days by 50% in 40–50% of patients with good tolerability. OnabotulinumtoxinA (Botox) injections every 12 weeks are approved for chronic migraine prevention (≥15 days/month).

Chronic pain affects 20% of adults globally and is the leading cause of disability. Multimodal pain management combines pharmacological and non-pharmacological approaches. WHO analgesic ladder: Step 1 (mild pain): paracetamol and/or NSAIDs; Step 2 (moderate): weak opioids (codeine, tramadol) + non-opioid; Step 3 (severe): strong opioids (morphine, oxycodone, hydromorphone). Adjuvants at any step: antidepressants (amitriptyline, duloxetine) and anticonvulsants (gabapentin, pregabalin) for neuropathic pain components.

Opioid prescribing for chronic non-cancer pain has declined following recognition of risks: addiction, overdose, hyperalgesia, and endocrine dysfunction. CDC 2022 guidelines recommend: use lowest effective dose, avoid >90 MME/day, co-prescribe naloxone for high-risk patients, reassess benefits/risks every 1–4 weeks when initiating and regularly thereafter. Non-opioid alternatives: NSAIDs, duloxetine (neuropathic, musculoskeletal), pregabalin/gabapentin (neuropathic), topical agents (lidocaine, diclofenac), interventional approaches (nerve blocks, spinal cord stimulation), and psychological therapies (CBT, ACT).

Adult immunisation schedule (US CDC/ACIP 2024 recommendations): Annual influenza vaccine for all adults. COVID-19 updated vaccine annually. Tdap once then Td every 10 years. Pneumococcal: PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23 for adults ≥65 or high-risk. Shingles: Shingrix (recombinant zoster vaccine) 2-dose series for adults ≥50 — 90%+ efficacy, superior to live Zostavax. HPV vaccine through age 26 routinely, 27–45 by shared decision. MMR if no evidence of immunity. Hepatitis B 3-dose series for unvaccinated adults.
source: Adult Immunisation Schedule — PMID 31774989 | Ann Intern Med. 2024

text: Dietary patterns associated with reduced cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, and all-cause mortality: Mediterranean diet (olive oil, fish, legumes, whole grains, nuts, moderate wine) reduces CVD events by 30% (PREDIMED trial). DASH diet reduces systolic BP by 8–11 mmHg. Plant-based diets reduce T2DM risk by 23%. Ultra-processed food consumption (>4 servings/day) is associated with 62% higher all-cause mortality. Dietary fibre 25–38 g/day reduces colorectal cancer risk by 10% per 10g increment. Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA + DHA 1–2 g/day) reduce triglycerides by 25–30%.
source: Dietary Patterns and Chronic Disease — PMID 31617577 | NEJM. 2019

text: Insomnia disorder affects 10–15% of adults chronically, with 30% experiencing occasional symptoms. DSM-5 criteria: difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or early awakening ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months causing significant distress. Cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia (CBT-I) is first-line treatment, superior to pharmacotherapy long-term, achieving remission in 80% of patients. Components: sleep restriction therapy, stimulus control, sleep hygiene education, relaxation training, cognitive restructuring. Digital CBT-I programs (Sleepio, Somryst) provide scalable access.

Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) affects 1 billion people worldwide, defined by ≥5 obstructive respiratory events/hour (AHI). Symptoms: snoring, witnessed apnoeas, nocturnal gasping, excessive daytime sleepiness (Epworth ≥10). Diagnosis by polysomnography or home sleep apnoea test. CPAP therapy is first-line, reducing AHI by >90% and improving daytime sleepiness, blood pressure (−2.5/−1.5 mmHg), and cardiovascular risk. Mandibular advancement devices are effective alternatives for mild-moderate OSA. Positional therapy for positional OSA (AHI worse supine). Weight loss of 10% reduces AHI by 26%.
source: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea Management — PMID 32160948 | Lancet. 2020

text: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD/GERD) affects 10–30% of Western populations. Typical symptoms: heartburn, regurgitation. Alarm features warranting urgent endoscopy: dysphagia, odynophagia, weight loss, anaemia, age >55 with new-onset symptoms. Lifestyle modifications: weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, avoiding late meals, reducing trigger foods (fatty foods, caffeine, alcohol, citrus, chocolate). Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs: omeprazole, lansoprazole, pantoprazole) are first-line pharmacotherapy, achieving symptom control in 80–90%. Long-term PPI use: risk of C. difficile, hypomagnesaemia, B12 deficiency — step-down to lowest effective dose.
source: GERD Management Guidelines — PMID 32427279 | Am J Gastroenterol. 2022

text: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) affects 11% of the global population and is the most common functional GI disorder. Rome IV criteria: recurrent abdominal pain ≥1 day/week for ≥3 months, associated with ≥2 of: defecation, change in stool frequency, change in stool form. Subtypes: IBS-C (constipation), IBS-D (diarrhoea), IBS-M (mixed). Pathophysiology involves gut-brain axis dysregulation, visceral hypersensitivity, altered gut microbiome, and intestinal permeability. Low FODMAP diet reduces symptoms in 50–80% of IBS-D patients.

Low back pain (LBP) is the leading cause of disability worldwide, affecting 540 million people. 90% of acute LBP resolves within 6 weeks with conservative management. Red flags requiring urgent imaging: cauda equina syndrome (bilateral leg weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anaesthesia), fever with back pain, unexplained weight loss, progressive neurological deficit, age >50 with new onset, history of malignancy. Routine imaging in non-specific LBP is not recommended and can increase unnecessary procedures.

First-line management of non-specific low back pain: remain active and reassurance (avoid bed rest), paracetamol and/or NSAIDs for short-term pain relief, supervised exercise therapy (core strengthening, yoga, tai chi), and psychological therapies (CBT) for chronic LBP with psychosocial yellow flags. Spinal manipulation provides modest short-term benefit. Opioids should not be used as first-line; reserved only for severe acute LBP short-term. Epidural corticosteroid injections provide short-term relief for radicular pain but no long-term benefit. Spinal surgery benefits carefully selected patients with structural pathology.
source: Low Back Pain Management Guidelines — PMID 30064271 | JAMA. 2018

text: Obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) affects 650 million adults globally and is a major risk factor for T2DM, CVD, hypertension, OSA, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and multiple cancers. Aetiology: complex interaction of genetic predisposition, environment, psychology, and socioeconomic factors — not simply lack of willpower. Weight loss of 5–10% significantly improves metabolic comorbidities. Structured lifestyle interventions combining dietary modification, physical activity (150–300 min/week moderate intensity), and behavioural therapy achieve 5–8 kg weight loss at 12 months.

Pharmacotherapy for obesity: semaglutide 2.4 mg/week (Wegovy) achieves 14.9% body weight reduction at 68 weeks in the STEP-1 trial — the most effective anti-obesity medication available. Tirzepatide (GLP-1/GIP dual agonist, Zepbound) achieves 20–22% weight loss at 72 weeks (SURMOUNT-1), approaching bariatric surgery outcomes. Older agents: orlistat (5% weight loss), naltrexone-bupropion, phentermine-topiramate. Bariatric surgery (sleeve gastrectomy, Roux-en-Y gastric bypass) achieves 25–35% weight loss and remission of T2DM in 60–80% of patients, with durable long-term outcomes.

Evidence-based cancer screening recommendations (USPSTF 2024): Colorectal cancer: colonoscopy every 10 years or FIT annually, ages 45–75. Breast cancer: mammography every 2 years, ages 40–74. Cervical cancer: Pap smear every 3 years or HPV test every 5 years, ages 21–65. Lung cancer: annual low-dose CT, ages 50–80, ≥20 pack-year history, current or quit within 15 years. Prostate cancer (PSA): shared decision-making ages 55–69. Skin cancer: no routine screening evidence in general population. Abdominal aortic aneurysm: one-time ultrasound for male smokers ages 65–75.
source: USPSTF Cancer Screening Recommendations — PMID 33961374 | JAMA. 2021

text: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) results from autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells, causing absolute insulin deficiency. Onset typically before age 30 but can occur at any age. GAD65, IA-2, and ZnT8 autoantibodies confirm autoimmune aetiology. Management: multiple daily injections (MDI) using basal-bolus regimen (long-acting basal: glargine, detemir, degludec + rapid-acting bolus: aspart, lispro, glulisine) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII/pump). Target HbA1c <53 mmol/mol (7%) balancing hypoglycaemia risk. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) significantly reduces HbA1c and hypoglycaemia.

Closed-loop insulin delivery systems (artificial pancreas) automatically adjust insulin delivery based on continuous glucose monitoring data using control algorithms. The MiniMed 780G and Omnipod 5 systems achieve time-in-range (70–180 mg/dL) >70% in clinical trials, significantly reducing HbA1c and hypoglycaemia. Teplizumab, an anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody, delays progression to clinical T1DM by 2–3 years in at-risk individuals with two or more autoantibodies and dysglycaemia — the first disease-modifying therapy approved for T1DM (FDA 2022).
source: Advances in Type 1 Diabetes Technology — PMID 34289308 | NEJM. 2021
